news_summary: Today's news highlights progress in strengthening civic trust and ensuring human rights as artificial intelligence increasingly supports public management.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité d'aujourd'hui met en lumière les progrès réalisés pour renforcer la confiance des citoyens et garantir le respect des droits de l'homme, alors que l'intelligence artificielle joue un rôle de plus en plus important dans la gestion publique.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy destacan los avances logrados en el fortalecimiento de la confianza ciudadana y la garantía de los derechos humanos, a medida que la inteligencia artificial desempeña un papel cada vez más importante en la gestión pública.

articles.title: Press release - EP TODAY
articles.summary: Monday 27 April Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
Last-minute press briefing at 16:30

The Parliament’s Spokesperson and the Press Service will hold a press briefing on this week’s plenary session at 16:30. You can follow the last-minute briefing live.

Journalists wishing to take part and ask questions should please connect via Interactio.

Consent-based legal definition of rape

In a debate with Equality Commissioner Hadja Lahbib from around 18.00, MEPs are expected to urge the Commission to table legislation to establish a common definition of rape based on the absence of freely given, informed and revocable consent. MEPs will vote on the report prepared by the Civil Liberties and Women’s Rights Committees tomorrow at noon, and a press conference with the rapporteurs will follow at 17.00.

Kyriakos KLOSIDIS

(+32) 470 96 47 35

Polona TEDESKO

(+32) 470 88 42 82

@EP_GenderEqual

@EP_Justice

Financial literacy

Social media financial communicators (“finfluencers”) are now a primary source of financial information for younger people. From around 20.30, MEPs and Financial Services Commissioner Maria Luís Albuquerque will be discussing the need for minimum standards in this field, a high-risk environment for hidden advertising, conflicts of interest, misleading claims and scams. The report, to be put to a vote on Thursday, calls for age-appropriate financial education, to equip citizens to identify scams and think critically about online financial content.

Dorota KOLINSKA

(+32) 498 98 32 80

@EP_Economics

In brief

Protecting the EU’s financial interests. From around 19.00, MEPs and Commissioner Piotr Serafin will discuss the Commission’s 2024 annual report on protecting the EU’s financial interests and combating fraud. Parliament will vote on the report tomorrow.

EIB annual report. Immediately following the opening of the session, at around 17.30 MEPS and Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis will discuss the Budgetary Control Committee’s assessment of the activities of the European Investment Bank in 2024. The report will be put to a vote tomorrow.

Ocean diplomacy. A Fisheries Committee report on how ocean diplomacy can help boost the competitiveness of the EU fisheries and aquaculture sector is last on today’s agenda. MEPs will be joined by Commissioner Costas Kadis from around 21.00. The vote is scheduled for tomorrow.

Live coverage of the plenary session can be found on Parliament’s webstreaming site and on EbS+.

For detailed information on the session, please also see our newsletter.

Find more information regarding plenary.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the European Parliament's efforts to establish a consent-based legal definition of rape, which represents a significant human rights advancement with broad societal impact. It also addresses financial literacy to protect younger people from scams, which is another positive social initiative. The focus on a clear legislative push and upcoming votes adds substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament pushes for clear rape definition and boosts financial literacy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The European Parliament is meeting this week to discuss important issues, including how to better protect people's rights and help young people understand money matters.

**What happened** – On Monday, Members of the European Parliament (MEPs) debated with Equality Commissioner Hadja Lahbib about creating a clear, common definition of rape based on consent. They plan to vote on this new definition soon. Later, MEPs and Financial Services Commissioner Maria Luís Albuquerque talked about improving financial education, especially for young people who often get money advice from social media influencers. They want to set rules to stop scams and misleading ads online.

**Impact** – Defining rape clearly using consent means that laws across the European Union will be more consistent and focused on respecting people’s freedom to agree or say no. This can help protect victims and improve justice. Improving financial literacy aims to protect young people from online scams and false information, helping them make safer money choices.

**What's next step** – The Parliament will vote on both the consent-based rape definition and the financial literacy proposals in the coming days. If approved, these changes will guide new laws and education programs across the EU.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament is working to strengthen laws on sexual consent and improve money education to protect people’s rights and safety.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen avance sur la définition du viol basée sur le consentement et l’éducation financière
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Parlement européen se réunit cette semaine pour discuter de sujets importants, notamment comment mieux protéger les droits des personnes et aider les jeunes à comprendre la gestion de l’argent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lundi, les députés européens ont débattu avec la commissaire à l’Égalité, Hadja Lahbib, sur la création d’une définition claire et commune du viol basée sur le consentement. Ils prévoient de voter prochainement sur cette nouvelle définition. Plus tard, les députés et la commissaire aux Services financiers, Maria Luís Albuquerque, ont parlé de l’amélioration de l’éducation financière, surtout pour les jeunes qui reçoivent souvent des conseils financiers sur les réseaux sociaux. Ils veulent instaurer des règles pour lutter contre les arnaques et les publicités trompeuses en ligne.

**Impact** – Définir clairement le viol par le consentement signifie que les lois dans toute l’Union européenne seront plus cohérentes et respecteront mieux la liberté des personnes à dire oui ou non. Cela peut aider à protéger les victimes et améliorer la justice. Améliorer l’éducation financière vise à protéger les jeunes des arnaques en ligne et des fausses informations, pour qu’ils fassent des choix d’argent plus sûrs.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Parlement votera dans les prochains jours sur la définition du viol basée sur le consentement et sur les propositions d’éducation financière. Si elles sont approuvées, ces mesures guideront de nouvelles lois et programmes éducatifs dans l’UE.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Parlement européen travaille à renforcer les lois sur le consentement sexuel et à améliorer l’éducation financière pour protéger les droits et la sécurité des personnes.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo impulsa una definición clara de violación basada en el consentimiento y mejora la educación financiera
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Parlamento Europeo se reúne esta semana para tratar temas importantes, como proteger mejor los derechos de las personas y ayudar a los jóvenes a entender cómo manejar el dinero.

**Qué pasó** – El lunes, los diputados europeos debatieron con la Comisionada de Igualdad, Hadja Lahbib, sobre crear una definición común y clara de violación basada en el consentimiento. Planean votar esta nueva definición pronto. Más tarde, los diputados y la Comisionada de Servicios Financieros, Maria Luís Albuquerque, hablaron sobre mejorar la educación financiera, especialmente para los jóvenes que reciben consejos de dinero en redes sociales. Quieren establecer reglas para evitar estafas y publicidad engañosa en línea.

**Impacto** – Definir la violación claramente según el consentimiento significa que las leyes en toda la Unión Europea serán más parecidas y respetarán mejor la libertad de las personas para decir sí o no. Esto puede ayudar a proteger a las víctimas y mejorar la justicia. Mejorar la educación financiera busca proteger a los jóvenes de estafas y falsas informaciones en línea, ayudándoles a tomar decisiones más seguras con su dinero.

**Próximo paso** – El Parlamento votará en los próximos días sobre la definición de violación basada en el consentimiento y las propuestas de educación financiera. Si se aprueban, estas medidas guiarán nuevas leyes y programas educativos en la UE.

**Resumen en una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo trabaja para fortalecer las leyes sobre el consentimiento sexual y mejorar la educación financiera para proteger los derechos y la seguridad de las personas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a balanced scale gently holding a glowing, transparent heart on one side and an open book with financial symbols on the other, set against a soft, natural background with subtle waves representing ocean diplomacy flowing beneath, all rendered in simple, earthy tones.

articles.title: The challenge of civic trust as AI plays a bigger role in public management
articles.summary: We have to insist that AI is still unsettled, still open to being shaped otherwise, and that the language and vocabulary it comes with, is not something we just accept.
articles.content:
This post is part of Global Voices’ April 2026 Spotlight series, “Human perspectives on AI.” This series will offer insight into how AI is being used in global majority countries, how its use and implementation are affecting individual communities, what this AI experiment might mean for future generations, and more. You can support this coverage by donating here.

I teach at and live on a public university campus in Hong Kong. At the beginning of every academic year, there is a steady stream of scam warnings: posters on campus, emails, student notices, reminders from the institution, posters splattered across MTR stations, and newspaper headlines. Be careful with suspicious links, fraudulent calls, fake payment requests, manipulated identities, messages that sound urgent and plausible enough to make you double-check. Especially after the headline-making incident in which an employee was duped by a deepfake video call with his company's financial chief into transferring millions, there has been increased and repeated messaging that AI is here to stay and that we are not prepared for it.

I find it ironic that one of the demographic groups most targeted by this message of potential AI-based scam is young students, who are often described as digital natives, instinctively literate in the languages of platforms, devices, and online life. Something has changed in our digital practices where caution has become the latest buzzword, because we can no longer trust whether what we see is what we get, and I do not think it is merely about the proliferation and persistence of fake news and misinformation. It is about the conditions of trust: not only our capacity to trust the digital information we encounter, but our capacity to trust our own judgment in everyday AI interactions.

Earlier debates about digital misinformation often focused on circulation: a rumour spreading too quickly, a manipulated image going viral, a false claim taking hold before it could be corrected. Those were serious problems, but they still assumed that falsehood was something inserted into an otherwise readable environment. With generative AI, we have entered a space where plausibility, not falsehood, has become cheap, scalable, ambient, and normal. It floods the digital with things that are not obviously fake, but also not reliably true, producing a kind of cognitive fatigue and surrender.

Human collectives can survive disagreement, but we have not yet figured out how to live with continuous distortion. What is being eroded is not only trust in the information we encounter, but trust in our own capacity to deal with and negotiate these generative AI plausibilities. Hong Kong’s recent emphasis on anti-fraud measures shows that deepfake scams and other forms of digitally mediated deception have become serious enough that the response now includes technical systems, such as scameter+, designed to identify or interrupt them. Financial authorities have openly described this as a new terrain in which AI must be used to fight AI. It means that trust is no longer restored by human recognition, but increasingly depends on technical verification layered on top of technical production.

This is the civic paradox at the heart of the current moment. The more synthetic and artificial plausibility overwhelms everyday judgment, the more verification becomes a specialised service. Institutions of reliability, or persons of trust known to us through proximity and relationality, are being replaced by a stack of systems that claim to tell us what is authentic. When a voice needs forensic checking, a message needs a platform signal, and a public interaction has to pass through a chatbot, a filter, a detection layer, and only then return as something we are meant to accept as reliable, civic trust begins to migrate away from shared social processes and into managed technical infrastructures.

While much of this is dealt with at the level of fraud detection and prevention, the consequences are far broader. Consider what happens when AI becomes embedded in the ordinary interfaces of public life: enquiry systems, service kiosks, governance application processes, translation tools, payment gateways, automated assistance, and a wider rollout of AI across the workflows of everyday management of the city. The presence of AI is justified through a reorganisation around speed, legibility, and efficiency, things that Hong Kong characteristically folds into its image as a dense, high-speed, hyper-connected city of neon-hued optimisation.

However, in this new scenario, where AI systems hold the power to verify and manage our interactions with each other and the city, civic encounters become increasingly processual and procedural. The citizen becomes a user. The public question becomes a query. And in the current policy direction for building a smart city, AI becomes central to an accelerated and expanded public-service delivery system. But efficiency, on its own, does not create civic trust. A public institution does not become more civic merely because it becomes more automated.

As is becoming apparent in the public hearings investigating the disaster of the fire in a residential estate in Tai Po, civic trust requires people to understand how decisions are made, where errors can be challenged, and how responsibility is distributed when something goes wrong. The challenge of civic trust in the face of AI is going to be temporal as much as technical. Calls for AI literacy, fluency, and caution are only going to be effective if we also change our public vocabularies for describing what these systems do: For instance, if we start calling AI systems systems of decision making rather than systems of automation, or think about input as capture, it changes how we relate to these technologies. Because by the time a harm is visible, the system already seems to have moved. In work around technology-facilitated gender and sexual violence, we know that by the time a synthetic image is debunked, the social effect has already travelled. By the time safeguards are written, the interface has already been normalised. We are always catching up. And this catching up produces a collective mistrust in our own ability to recognise what is happening while it is happening.

Verification is often offered as the necessary solution to these questions, but it is insufficient. If civic life is reduced to an endless cycle of detection, correction, and response, then public trust becomes permanently defensive. We stop asking what kinds of relations technology should make possible, and instead settle for damage control.

What would it mean to think differently? At the Digital Narratives Studio, which I direct, we begin with a modest but important shift: moving from asking how AI can improve civic services to asking what kinds of civic relations those services should sustain. Efficiency and safety are necessary, but so are hesitation, explanation, and the possibility of remaining answerable to one another, even when our encounters are increasingly shaped by synthetic systems.

We call this relational infrastructuring. If trust is being displaced from shared social processes into technical systems of synthetic plausibility and verification, then the response cannot simply be better detection or louder warnings. I also do not hold a nostalgic wish to return to a time before technological mediations, as if that were even real. We have to stay with the technologies that now organize civic life, but refuse to accept the narratives of decision-making and trust that they offer. For us, it means creating embodied, shared processes through which people can name what they are encountering, compare experiences, share interpretations, test judgment together, and build a collective vocabulary for the new conditions that AI has normalized.

In our work, this takes the form of small experiments in language, relation, and possibility. Through informal and semi-structured gatherings, always over food, we create spaces where people can describe how AI-driven systems enter their everyday lives, and where new civic vocabularies can emerge from lived experience rather than technical prescription alone. I do not want to prescribe a language as if there is one settled one, but do try and think about how you would understand the futures of AI, the histories they consolidate, and the experience they unsettle if you had to incorporate terms like the unsettled, entanglement, dirty mirror, toxic intimacy, grief, and companionship as a part of describing AI in your everyday life. We extend this into practices of relational language modelling — a practice that doesn't mimic machine correlations but human relationship building as a way of shaping and interpreting the data set, where human intention, social context, mutual accountability, and shared anxiety can be named, and become central to how we imagine civic engagement with AI. And we pair this with a commitment possibility: insisting that AI is still unsettled, still open to being shaped otherwise, and that the language and vocabulary it comes with, is not something we just accept.

This bottom-up approach is not scalable or easy, but it is grounded in the idea that we live with and through technologies and don’t merely stand outside and critique them. We think through rebuilding the relational conditions within which technologies are encountered, interpreted and made accountable so that we are not merely looking to verify what is real, but to create publics capable of making meaning together.
articles.reasoning: The article addresses the significant societal challenge of maintaining civic trust in the era of AI-driven public management. It highlights a positive, thoughtful approach to fostering collective understanding, accountability, and relational engagement with AI technologies rather than mere technical verification. The focus on building new civic vocabularies and shared processes for interpreting AI's role in public life has broad societal relevance and offers a hopeful, constructive path forward.
articles.personality_title: Building trust with AI in public life through shared understanding
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As artificial intelligence (AI) becomes more common in public services, people face new challenges in trusting these systems. In Hong Kong, for example, warnings about AI-based scams have increased, showing how tricky it is to know what information is true or fake.

**What happened** – A teacher in Hong Kong explains how AI is changing the way people interact with public services and information. Instead of just focusing on detecting fake AI content, their group creates meetings where people can talk about how AI affects their daily lives. They work on building new words and ways to understand AI together, helping people trust each other and the technology.

**Impact** – This approach is important because it moves beyond just trying to catch mistakes or scams. It helps people learn how AI works in the real world and how to question it safely. This shared understanding can make public services better and more trustworthy, rather than just faster or more automated.

**What's next step** – The group plans to continue these small community gatherings to explore how AI changes public life. They want to help more people feel confident using AI systems and to shape AI in ways that support real relationships and fairness in society.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Building trust in AI-driven public services requires new ways for people to talk, understand, and share experiences about how AI shapes their lives.
articles.personality_title_fr: Construire la confiance avec l’IA dans la vie publique grâce à une compréhension partagée
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Alors que l’intelligence artificielle (IA) devient plus présente dans les services publics, les gens rencontrent de nouveaux défis pour faire confiance à ces systèmes. À Hong Kong, par exemple, les avertissements concernant les arnaques liées à l’IA se multiplient, montrant combien il est difficile de distinguer le vrai du faux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un enseignant à Hong Kong explique comment l’IA change la manière dont les personnes interagissent avec les services publics et l’information. Au lieu de se concentrer uniquement sur la détection des contenus faux générés par l’IA, son groupe organise des rencontres où les gens peuvent parler de l’impact de l’IA dans leur vie quotidienne. Ils travaillent à créer de nouveaux mots et moyens de comprendre l’IA ensemble, aidant ainsi les gens à se faire confiance mutuellement et à faire confiance à la technologie.

**Impact** – Cette approche est importante car elle dépasse la simple détection des erreurs ou des arnaques. Elle aide les gens à comprendre comment l’IA fonctionne dans la réalité et comment la questionner en toute sécurité. Cette compréhension partagée peut rendre les services publics meilleurs et plus dignes de confiance, plutôt que simplement plus rapides ou automatisés.

**Prochaine étape** – Le groupe prévoit de poursuivre ces petits rassemblements communautaires pour explorer comment l’IA transforme la vie publique. Ils souhaitent aider plus de personnes à se sentir en confiance avec les systèmes d’IA et à façonner l’IA de manière à soutenir de véritables relations et l’équité dans la société.

**Phrase clé** – Construire la confiance dans les services publics pilotés par l’IA nécessite de nouvelles façons pour les gens de parler, comprendre et partager leurs expériences sur le rôle de l’IA dans leur vie.
articles.personality_title_es: Construir confianza con la IA en la vida pública mediante la comprensión compartida
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que la inteligencia artificial (IA) se vuelve más común en los servicios públicos, las personas enfrentan nuevos desafíos para confiar en estos sistemas. En Hong Kong, por ejemplo, han aumentado las alertas sobre estafas basadas en IA, mostrando lo difícil que es saber qué información es verdadera o falsa.

**Qué pasó** – Un profesor en Hong Kong explica cómo la IA está cambiando la forma en que las personas interactúan con los servicios públicos y la información. En lugar de centrarse solo en detectar contenido falso generado por IA, su grupo organiza reuniones donde la gente puede hablar sobre cómo la IA afecta su vida diaria. Trabajan en crear nuevas palabras y formas de entender la IA juntos, ayudando a que las personas confíen entre sí y en la tecnología.

**Impacto** – Este enfoque es importante porque va más allá de solo atrapar errores o estafas. Ayuda a las personas a aprender cómo funciona la IA en la vida real y cómo cuestionarla de manera segura. Esta comprensión compartida puede hacer que los servicios públicos sean mejores y más confiables, no solo más rápidos o automatizados.

**Próximo paso** – El grupo planea continuar con estas pequeñas reuniones comunitarias para explorar cómo la IA cambia la vida pública. Quieren ayudar a más personas a sentirse seguras usando sistemas de IA y a moldear la IA de maneras que apoyen relaciones reales y justicia en la sociedad.

**Frase clave** – Construir confianza en los servicios públicos impulsados por IA requiere nuevas formas para que las personas hablen, entiendan y compartan experiencias sobre cómo la IA influye en sus vidas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an open, softly glowing network of interlocking hands made of translucent light, gently weaving together with delicate, floating digital threads and softly pulsing nodes, symbolizing human connection and trust being rebuilt through shared dialogue and collaborative understanding amid an abstract, calm cityscape background rendered in natural, muted tones.
